items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.title: Understanding Hemolysis in Hemolytic Anemias (HAs)
items.content: In hemolytic anemias, the primary issue is increased destruction of red cells rather than decreased production or acute blood loss. This results in overconsumption of red cells from peripheral circulation while bone marrow supply remains normal or elevated.
keyPoints: Anemia results from overconsumption of red cells from peripheral blood.
Bone marrow supply is usually increased, not diminished.
Differentiation from other anemias: signs and symptoms arise directly from hemolysis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.title: Clinical Presentation of Hemolytic Anemias
items.content: The clinical presentation of HAs can vary significantly based on the onset and severity. Acute cases like autoimmune HA or favism may present as medical emergencies, while chronic conditions such as hereditary spherocytosis (HS) or cold agglutinin disease (CAD) might be diagnosed after years.
keyPoints: Clinical presentation influenced by abrupt vs. gradual onset.
Autoimmune HA and favism can be acute medical emergencies.
Mild HS and CAD may present chronically.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.title: Laboratory Features of Hemolytic Anemias
items.content: Laboratory features in HAs are characterized by signs of hemolysis and the bone marrow's erythropoietic response. Extravascular hemolysis increases unconjugated bilirubin, AST, and urobilinogen, while intravascular hemolysis is marked by hemoglobinuria, increased LDH, and reduced haptoglobin.
keyPoints: Extravascular hemolysis: increased unconjugated bilirubin, AST, and urobilinogen.
Intravascular hemolysis: hemoglobinuria, increased LDH, reduced haptoglobin.
Erythropoietic response: increase in reticulocytes (percentage and absolute count), increased MCV.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.title: Red Cell Metabolic Limitations
items.content: Mature red cells have limited metabolic capabilities due to the loss of organelles and biosynthetic abilities during differentiation.
keyPoints: Final hemoglobin level in mature red cells is approximately 340 g/L (5 mM).
Red cells rely solely on anaerobic glycolysis for ATP production, lacking oxidative phosphorylation backup due to the loss of mitochondria.
Protein synthesis is lost with ribosome degradation; thus, damaged proteins cannot be replaced, leading to enzyme activity decline as red cells age.

items.title: Senescence and Phagocytosis of Red Cells
items.content: Aging red cells become denser, exposing band 3 antigens that lead to opsonization and subsequent phagocytosis.
keyPoints: Band 3 is the most abundant protein in the red cell membrane with about 1.2 million molecules per cell.
Senescent red cells are recognized by low-avidity anti-band 3 IgG antibodies, enhancing opsonization and phagocytosis by macrophages.

items.title: Consequences of Increased Red Cell Turnover
items.content: Increased red cell turnover in hemolytic anemias leads to various clinical consequences, including iron metabolism issues.
keyPoints: Red cell life span is reduced from the normal ~120 days in hemolytic disorders.
Chronic intravascular hemolysis can cause significant iron loss due to hemoglobinuria.
Chronic extravascular hemolysis often results in iron overload, particularly with frequent blood transfusions.

items.title: Compensated Hemolysis: Understanding Erythropoiesis Balance
items.content: In compensated hemolysis, the bone marrow's increased red cell production balances heightened red cell destruction. This balance is maintained by erythropoietin (EPO) produced in response to hemolysis. Clinically, this means a patient may present without anemia despite underlying hemolytic conditions.
keyPoints: Compensated hemolysis occurs when increased erythropoiesis balances red cell destruction.
Erythropoietin (EPO) mediates the bone marrow's response to hemolysis.
Patients with compensated hemolysis may not exhibit anemia.
Decompensation can occur under stressors like pregnancy, folate deficiency, or renal failure.

items.title: Impact of Intercurrent Conditions on Chronic Hemolytic Anemias
items.content: In chronic hemolytic anemias, intercurrent conditions like infections can significantly depress erythropoiesis. This leads to a more pronounced drop in hemoglobin levels compared to individuals without hemolysis. Parvovirus B19 infection is a notable example that can precipitate an aplastic crisis.
keyPoints: Intercurrent conditions can exacerbate the effects of chronic hemolytic anemias.
Erythropoiesis depression during such events leads to marked drops in hemoglobin.
Parvovirus B19 infection is a classic cause of aplastic crises in hemolytic anemias.

items.title: Understanding Hereditary Spherocytosis: Prevalence and Genetic Heterogeneity
items.content: Hereditary spherocytosis (HS) is the most common inherited hemolytic anemia among populations of European ancestry, with a prevalence estimated at 1:2000–1:5000. It was first identified by Minkowsky and Chauffard in the late nineteenth century due to the presence of spherocytes in peripheral blood. HS is characterized by genetic heterogeneity, meaning it can arise from mutations in several genes. The inheritance pattern of HS varies; while often autosomal dominant, severe forms may be autosomal recessive.
keyPoints: HS has a prevalence of 1:2000–1:5000 among European populations.
First identified by Minkowsky and Chauffard due to spherocytes in blood.
Genetically heterogeneous; can arise from mutations in several genes.
Inheritance is often autosomal dominant but severe forms may be autosomal recessive.

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Hereditary Spherocytosis
items.content: The clinical severity of hereditary spherocytosis can vary widely, from severe anemia presenting in infancy to mild cases appearing in young adults or later. Key clinical findings include jaundice, splenomegaly (an enlarged spleen), and the presence of gallstones, which may prompt diagnostic investigations in younger individuals.
keyPoints: Clinical severity ranges from severe anemia in infancy to mild cases in adulthood.
Key findings: jaundice, splenomegaly, and gallstones.

items.title: Molecular Basis of Hereditary Spherocytosis
items.content: The variability in clinical manifestations among patients with hereditary spherocytosis is largely due to different underlying molecular lesions. HS involves mutations in several genes, and the disorder can arise from different mutations within the same gene, complicating a one-to-one correlation between specific genes and the disorder.
keyPoints: Clinical variability is due to different molecular lesions.
HS involves mutations in multiple genes.
Different mutations of the same gene can lead to HS.

items.title: Diagnosing Hereditary Spherocytosis: Key Laboratory Indicators
items.content: Hereditary spherocytosis (HS) can be suspected based on specific blood count parameters. An increased mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC >34 g/dL) and an elevated red cell distribution width (RDW >14%) with normal or slightly decreased MCV are indicative of HS.
keyPoints: Increased MCHC (>34 g/dL)
Elevated RDW (>14%)
Normal or slightly decreased MCV

items.title: Role of the Spleen in Hereditary Spherocytosis and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: The spleen contributes to HS pathology by increasing red cell destruction due to reduced deformability, leading to vesiculation. Diagnosis can be challenging without a family history; it may require specialized tests like osmotic fragility, the acid glycerol lysis test, EMA-binding test, SDS-gel electrophoresis, and ektacytometry.
keyPoints: Spleen's role in red cell destruction
Tests for diagnosis: osmotic fragility, acid glycerol lysis, EMA-binding, SDS-gel electrophoresis, ektacytometry

items.title: Splenectomy Timing and Vaccination in Hereditary Spherocytosis
items.content: In hereditary spherocytosis (HS), splenectomy is a key treatment strategy due to the spleen's role in red cell destruction. The timing of splenectomy varies based on disease severity: severe cases undergo splenectomy at 4–6 years, moderate cases delay until puberty, and mild cases avoid it. Pre-splenectomy vaccination against encapsulated bacteria (Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae) is crucial.
keyPoints: Splenectomy benefits HS by reducing red cell destruction.
Timing: 4–6 years for severe cases, at puberty for moderate cases; avoid in mild cases.
Pre-splenectomy vaccination against Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae is imperative.

items.title: Management of Severe Hereditary Spherocytosis Cases
items.content: In severe HS cases, which are estimated to be less than 10% and transfusion dependent, erythropoietin may help reduce the need for transfusions in infants. Splenectomy is generally beneficial, but partial splenectomy can be considered based on family history outcomes.
keyPoints: <10% of HS cases are severe and transfusion dependent.
Erythropoietin may be transfusion sparing in infants with severe HS.
Partial splenectomy can be an option depending on familial outcomes.

items.title: Clinical Features and Management of Hereditary Pyropoikilocytosis
items.content: Hereditary pyropoikilocytosis (HPP), a severe form of hereditary elliptocytosis, involves significant red cell cytoskeleton instability. Patients exhibit decreased mean corpuscular volume (MCV: 50–60 fL) and splenomegaly. Splenectomy is often beneficial for these patients.
keyPoints: HPP involves biallelic mutations leading to severe hemolytic anemia.
Characteristic MCV in HPP is decreased, ranging from 50–60 fL.
Splenectomy can be beneficial due to splenomegaly and severe hemolysis.

items.title: Splenectomy Contraindication in Stomatocytosis
items.content: Splenectomy should be avoided in patients with stomatocytosis due to the high risk of severe thromboembolic complications observed post-splenectomy.
keyPoints: Stomatocytosis is associated with a significant risk of severe thromboembolic events after splenectomy.
Splenectomy is contraindicated in stomatocytosis.

items.title: Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Pyruvate kinase deficiency, an autosomal recessive disorder, presents with hemolytic anemia that can manifest severely in newborns, often requiring phototherapy or exchange transfusion. The condition may remain undiagnosed until exacerbation during pregnancy.
keyPoints: Prevalence of pyruvate kinase deficiency is approximately 1:10,000.
E277K polymorphism found with heterozygote frequencies of 1–7% in some African populations.
Clinical presentation includes severe neonatal jaundice and variable severity of anemia.
Increased 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate levels enhance oxygen delivery despite low hemoglobin.

items.title: Management of Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency: Supportive Care and Emerging Therapies
items.content: Supportive management for pyruvate kinase (PK) deficiency includes folic acid supplementation, blood transfusions as needed, and iron chelation to prevent overload. Splenectomy can modestly increase hemoglobin levels by releasing trapped reticulocytes from the spleen. Mitapivat, an allosteric activator of PK, has shown promise in increasing hemoglobin in patients with specific missense mutations.
keyPoints: Folic acid supplements should be given constantly.
Blood transfusion and iron chelation may be necessary to manage symptoms and prevent complications.
Splenectomy can increase hemoglobin levels by releasing trapped reticulocytes, though it paradoxically increases reticulocyte count.
Mitapivat is effective in increasing hemoglobin in patients with specific missense mutations of PKLR.

items.title: Splenectomy and Its Effects on Hemoglobin Levels in Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency
items.content: In pyruvate kinase deficiency, splenectomy is performed to increase hemoglobin levels by releasing reticulocytes trapped in the spleen. This procedure often results in a modest but significant rise in hemoglobin and an increase in reticulocyte count.
keyPoints: About one-half of patients undergo splenectomy for increased hemoglobin levels.
Splenectomy releases reticulocytes from the spleen, increasing both hemoglobin and reticulocyte counts.

items.title: Emerging Therapies: Mitapivat for Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency
items.content: Mitapivat, an allosteric activator of pyruvate kinase, is a novel therapeutic option that has been approved based on its ability to significantly increase hemoglobin levels in patients with specific missense mutations of the PKLR gene.
keyPoints: Mitapivat is the first drug for a red cell enzymopathy, specifically targeting pyruvate kinase deficiency.
It increases hemoglobin levels in about one-half of patients with missense mutations.

items.title: X-Linked Inheritance of G6PD Deficiency
items.content: G6PD deficiency is X-linked, affecting males and females differently. Males are hemizygous, so they are either normal or deficient, whereas females can be normal, heterozygous (intermediate), or homozygous (deficient). Heterozygous females exhibit a variable clinical expression due to X chromosome inactivation.
keyPoints: G6PD gene is X-linked.
Males are hemizygous; they can be normal or deficient.
Females can be normal, heterozygous (intermediate), or homozygous (deficient).
Heterozygous females show variable clinical expression due to X chromosome inactivation.

items.title: Genetic Heterogeneity and Clinical Impact of G6PD Deficiency
items.content: G6PD deficiency involves over 230 different mutations, primarily missense point mutations affecting protein stability or catalytic function. This genetic heterogeneity results in varying clinical severities, with some variants causing chronic nonspherocytic hemolytic anemia (CNSHA) due to severe enzyme instability.
keyPoints: Over 230 different G6PD gene mutations exist.
Most are single missense point mutations affecting protein stability or function.
Genetic heterogeneity leads to varying clinical severities, including CNSHA.

items.title: Epidemiology and Evolutionary Advantage of G6PD Deficiency
items.content: G6PD deficiency is prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions, affecting at least 500 million people globally. It confers a selective advantage against Plasmodium falciparum malaria, particularly in heterozygous females, illustrating balanced polymorphism.
keyPoints: Affects at least 500 million people worldwide.
Prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions (Africa, southern Europe, Middle East, Southeast Asia, Oceania).
Confers resistance against Plasmodium falciparum malaria, especially in heterozygous females.

items.title: Triggers and Presentation of AHA in G6PD Deficiency
items.content: Acute hemolytic anemia (AHA) in G6PD deficiency can be triggered by fava beans, infections, or drugs. The onset is abrupt with symptoms including malaise, weakness, abdominal pain, jaundice, and dark urine. Anemia is moderate to severe, normocytic, and normochromic due to intravascular hemolysis.
keyPoints: Triggers: fava beans, infections, drugs
Symptoms: malaise, weakness, abdominal pain, jaundice, dark urine
Anemia characteristics: moderate to severe, normocytic and normochromic
Associated findings: hemoglobinemia, hemoglobinuria, high LDH, low or absent plasma haptoglobin
Blood film features: anisocytosis, polychromasia, spherocytes, bizarre poikilocytes, bite cells

items.title: Management and Risks of PQ in G6PD Deficiency
items.content: Primaquine is used for eliminating P. falciparum gametocytes and Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites, with a single dose being safe for G6PD-deficient individuals. Mass administration should be paired with G6PD testing to prevent hemolysis.
keyPoints: Primaquine use: eliminates P. falciparum gametocytes and Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites
Dose: 0.25 mg/kg for gametocyte elimination
Safety: safe for G6PD-deficient persons with appropriate testing
Mass administration: requires G6PD testing

items.title: Chronic Non-Spherocytic Hemolytic Anemia in G6PD Deficiency
items.content: A minority with class A variant of G6PD deficiency suffer from chronic non-spherocytic hemolytic anemia (CNSHA), presenting later in life with symptoms like anemia, jaundice, or gallstones. These patients are susceptible to acute oxidative damage and bacterial infections due to impaired granulocyte function.
keyPoints: Class A variant: leads to CNSHA
Presentation: anemia, jaundice, gallstones, possible splenomegaly
Anemia characteristics: normo-macrocytic with reticulocytosis, increased bilirubin and LDH
Susceptibility: acute oxidative damage, bacterial infections due to impaired granulocyte function

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to G6PD Deficiency
items.content: G6PD deficiency diagnosis involves differentiating between screening and diagnostic tests. Screening tests are suitable for population studies, while diagnostic tests are essential during or after a hemolytic attack when young red cells with higher G6PD activity may skew results.
keyPoints: Screening tests classify males as normal or deficient in the steady state.
Diagnostic tests needed post-hemolysis to differentiate between normal and deficient hemizygotes in males; some heterozygous females at risk of hemolysis are identified.
Point-of-care tests for G6PD deficiency are becoming available, useful for mass administration of drugs like PQ or tafenoquine.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Prevention and Management of Hemolytic Episodes in G6PD Deficiency
items.content: Preventing hemolysis in G6PD deficiency involves avoiding known triggers such as fava beans and certain drugs. When acute hemolytic anemia occurs, management may include blood transfusions or dialysis if renal failure ensues.
keyPoints: Avoidance of triggering factors like fava beans prevents favism.
Drug-induced hemolysis can be prevented by testing for G6PD deficiency before prescribing drugs; alternative medications are often available.
Severe anemia may require blood transfusion, especially in children; renal failure necessitates dialysis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Management of Chronic and Severe Cases of G6PD-Related Anemia
items.content: Chronic management includes folic acid supplementation and hematologic surveillance, while severe cases may benefit from interventions like blood transfusions or splenectomy.
keyPoints: Regular folic acid supplements and hematologic monitoring are recommended for non-severe chronic hemolytic anemia (CNSHA).
Blood transfusions may be necessary during exacerbations, particularly with infections.
Splenectomy can be beneficial in severe cases, despite no evidence of selective red cell destruction in the spleen.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Familial Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (aHUS) and its Complement Pathway Involvement
items.content: Familial hemolytic-uremic syndrome (aHUS) is a rare disorder characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure. It results from dysregulation of the complement alternative pathway due to mutations in genes encoding proteins that are components or regulators of this system.
keyPoints: aHUS is caused by mutations affecting the complement alternative pathway, including genes like C3, CFB, CFH, CD46, CFI, and THBD.
Unlike other inherited hemolytic anemias (HAs), aHUS results from defects external to red cells.
The disease requires a triggering factor for clinical manifestation, often leading to incomplete penetrance.

items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of aHUS with Eculizumab
items.content: Eculizumab, an anti-C5 complement inhibitor, has significantly improved the treatment outcomes for aHUS by ameliorating microangiopathic symptoms and reducing the need for plasma exchange.
keyPoints: Before eculizumab, mortality in aHUS was up to 15% acutely, with up to 50% progressing to end-stage renal disease (ESRD).
Eculizumab can be discontinued after full remission is achieved within 3–6 months if urine dipstick tests negative for hemoglobinuria.
Relapses are possible; however, patients have responded well upon re-treatment with eculizumab.

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Inherited Hemolytic Anemias
items.content: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) is increasingly used for diagnosing inherited red cell abnormalities, offering a comprehensive and definitive approach.
keyPoints: NGS can identify mutations in genes associated with membrane-cytoskeleton disorders, channelopathies, and enzymopathies.
While NGS provides potential comprehensiveness, identified mutations may sometimes be of uncertain significance.
Conventional methodologies are still necessary to confirm diagnoses when mutation significance is unclear.

items.title: Mechanical Destruction of Red Cells
items.content: Red cells can undergo intravascular hemolysis due to mechanical destruction, leading to conditions like march hemoglobinuria and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. March hemoglobinuria occurs acutely in activities causing repeated foot impact, while chronic hemolysis is often iatrogenic, seen with prosthetic heart valves.
keyPoints: Intravascular hemolysis results from mechanical destruction of red cells.
March hemoglobinuria can occur after intense physical activity like marathon running or barefoot dancing.
Chronic microangiopathic hemolytic anemia is common in patients with prosthetic heart valves, especially with paraprosthetic regurgitation.

items.title: Infection-Related Hemolytic Anemias
items.content: Infections are a frequent cause of hemolytic anemia, with malaria being predominant in endemic areas. In other regions, Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 is common, particularly causing HUS in children. Clostridium perfringens can lead to life-threatening intravascular hemolysis.
keyPoints: Malaria is the most frequent infectious cause of HA in endemic areas.
Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 is a common direct cause of HUS, especially in children.
Clostridium perfringens can cause life-threatening intravascular hemolysis due to lecithinase activity.

items.title: Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA)
items.content: AIHA is characterized by autoantibodies against red cell antigens, with an incidence of 1-3:100,000 and prevalence of 17:100,000 in the U.S. It can present abruptly with severe symptoms like hemoglobin levels dropping to as low as 4 g/dL.
keyPoints: AIHA has an estimated incidence of 1-3:100,000 per year and a prevalence of 17:100,000.
Mortality in AIHA is approximately 5-10% despite appropriate management.
Clinical features include rapid onset with hemoglobin levels dropping to as low as 4 g/dL, jaundice, and possible splenomegaly.

items.title: Role and Sensitivity of the Direct Antiglobulin Test in AIHA Diagnosis
items.content: The direct antiglobulin test, developed by R. R. A. Coombs, is crucial for diagnosing autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA). It detects immunoglobulins or complement components on red cells. Advanced techniques can detect as few as 30–40 molecules per cell.
keyPoints: The test uses a broad-spectrum reagent to detect Ig and C components.
Positive results are almost diagnostic of AIHA, with sensitivity varying by technique.
Advanced methods like flow cytometry increase sensitivity to 30-40 molecules per red cell.
False positives can occur post-transfusion or organ transplantation.

items.title: Mechanism and Clinical Implications of AIHA
items.content: In AIHA, the autoantibody reacts optimally at 37°C, often targeting Rhesus antigens. Hemolysis occurs primarily in macrophage-rich areas like the spleen due to erythrophagocytosis.
keyPoints: Autoantibodies typically react best at 37°C and are often Rhesus specific.
Hemolysis is mediated by Fc receptor recognition on macrophages, leading to erythrophagocytosis.
The spleen is a primary site of red cell destruction due to its anatomy and macrophage abundance.
AIHA can be idiopathic or secondary to conditions like SLE, CLL, or post-transplant immunosuppression.

items.title: Management of Severe Acute AIHA
items.content: In severe acute AIHA, immediate treatment often involves transfusion of red cells despite potential compatibility issues. ABO-matched but incompatible blood may be used to maintain patient survival until medical treatment can take effect.
keyPoints: Severe acute AIHA is a medical emergency requiring prompt action.
Transfusion with ABO-matched but incompatible blood is justified as it ensures patient survival while awaiting remission from medical therapy.
Prednisone (1 mg/kg per day) should be initiated immediately if anemia is not life-threatening, achieving remission in at least half of patients.
Rituximab (100 mg/week × 4) combined with prednisone as first-line treatment reduces relapse rates.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Refractory or Relapsed AIHA
items.content: For patients who do not respond to initial treatments, splenectomy and rituximab are viable options. Splenectomy removes a major site of hemolysis but carries risks like sepsis and thrombosis.
keyPoints: Splenectomy does not cure AIHA but can improve anemia by reducing hemolysis.
Rituximab is increasingly used as first-line treatment, showing similar response rates to splenectomy.
Second- or third-line treatments include azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, mycophenolate, and intravenous immunoglobulin.
In severe refractory cases, high-dose cyclophosphamide (50 mg/kg per day for 4 days) followed by a myelostimulating agent or alemtuzumab may be considered.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.title: Additional Therapeutic Options and Considerations
items.content: Fostamatinib, an inhibitor of Syk kinase, has shown promise as a third-line treatment. Erythropoietin (EPO) may be used to reduce transfusion needs when severe anemia is associated with inadequate reticulocyte output.
keyPoints: Fostamatinib shows beneficial effects in treating refractory AIHA cases.
EPO can help manage severe anemia by reducing or avoiding the need for red cell transfusions.
Ongoing research includes inhibitors of the neonatal Fc receptor and other agents.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Cold Agglutinin Disease (CAD) Pathophysiology
items.content: Cold agglutinin disease is characterized by the production of IgM antibodies that react with red cells at lower temperatures, leading to hemolysis. This condition involves complement activation and subsequent intravascular and extravascular destruction of red cells.
keyPoints: IgM autoantibodies have an anti-I specificity and can reach high titers (1:100,000 or more).
The antibody activates the complement cascade, causing intravascular hemolysis.
C3b binding leads to extravascular hemolysis by macrophages, not predominantly in the spleen.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.title: Management of Cold Agglutinin Disease
items.content: Rituximab is a first-line treatment for CAD, especially effective in severe cases and those with lymphoproliferative disorders. Other treatments include plasma exchange, B-cell receptor inhibitors, and complement inhibitors like sutimlimab.
keyPoints: Up to 60% of patients respond to rituximab, often used as first-line treatment.
Combination therapy with fludarabine or bendamustine can enhance remission durability.
Complement inhibitor sutimlimab is effective but requires continuous administration.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.title: Supportive Treatment and Drug-Induced Hemolysis in CAD
items.content: Blood transfusions may be necessary for supportive care, with precautions to keep both the blood bag and patient warm. Additionally, various drugs can cause hemolysis through oxidative or nonoxidative mechanisms.
keyPoints: Transfused red cells should be kept warm due to their short survival in CAD patients.
Chemicals like hyperbaric oxygen and dapsone can induce hemolysis even without G6PD deficiency.
Lead poisoning causes hemolytic anemia with basophilic stippling, mimicking pyruvate kinase (P5N) deficiency.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.title: Drug-Induced Warm Antibody AIHA
items.content: Certain drugs can induce warm antibody autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) through mechanisms such as mimicry or hapten formation. Methyldopa, for example, can stimulate the production of the Rhesus antibody anti-e in patients with the e antigen.
keyPoints: Drug-induced AIHA can occur via mimicry or hapten formation.
Methyldopa can induce the production of anti-e antibodies in susceptible individuals.
AIHA typically subsides after discontinuation of the offending drug.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria
items.content: PNH is characterized by persistent intravascular hemolysis, pancytopenia, and a tendency to venous thrombosis. It can present with symptoms like passing blood instead of urine or as anemia with possible neutropenia or thrombocytopenia.
keyPoints: PNH presents with hemolysis, pancytopenia, and venous thrombosis.
Classic presentation includes nocturnal hemoglobinuria (passing blood instead of urine).
Anemia may be normo-macrocytic with reticulocytosis up to 20% or 400,000/μL.
Unconjugated bilirubin and LDH are elevated; haptoglobin is usually undetectable.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.title: Laboratory Findings in PNH
items.content: PNH diagnosis involves identifying anemia with normo-macrocytosis, elevated unconjugated bilirubin and LDH, undetectable haptoglobin, and possible hemoglobinuria. Bone marrow shows erythroid hyperplasia, sometimes with dyserythropoietic features.
keyPoints: Anemia in PNH is usually normo-macrocytic with marked reticulocytosis.
Elevated unconjugated bilirubin and LDH levels are typical; haptoglobin is undetectable.
Bone marrow shows erythroid hyperplasia, sometimes dyserythropoietic features.
PNH can lead to bone marrow becoming hypocellular or aplastic.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.title: Definitive Diagnosis and Gold Standard for Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)
items.content: The definitive diagnosis of PNH relies on identifying a substantial proportion of red cells deficient in CD59 and CD55, which protect against complement-mediated hemolysis. Flow cytometry is the gold standard due to its high sensitivity, revealing a bimodal distribution of cells with distinct populations lacking these proteins.
keyPoints: Definitive diagnosis requires demonstration of substantial deficiency in CD59 and CD55 on red cells.
Flow cytometry is the gold standard for diagnosing PNH.
A population of at least 5% CD59(-) red cells and 20% CD59(-) granulocytes is needed for diagnosis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.title: Pathophysiology of PNH: Mechanisms of Hemolysis
items.content: Hemolysis in PNH primarily occurs intravascularly due to red cell sensitivity to activated complement, stemming from deficiencies in protective proteins like CD59. This deficiency is linked to a somatic mutation in the PIGA gene affecting GPI biosynthesis, leading to a mosaic of mutant and nonmutant cells.
keyPoints: Hemolysis is mainly intravascular due to red cell sensitivity to activated complement.
CD59 deficiency hinders insertion of C9 polymers (MAC) into the membrane.
PIGA gene mutation leads to GPI biosynthesis defect, resulting in PNH cells.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.title: Relationship Between PNH and Aplastic Anemia
items.content: PNH often follows a history of aplastic anemia (AA), reflecting shared pathogenic mechanisms. Both conditions may involve autoimmune processes targeting hematopoietic stem cells, with GPI-negative cells in PNH being spared due to negative selection against GPI-positive cells.
keyPoints: Patients with established PNH frequently have a prior history of AA.
PNH and AA share autoimmune pathogenesis involving T cell-mediated damage.
GPI-negative stem cells are spared in PNH, possibly due to negative selection by GPI-specific T cells.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.title: Eculizumab as a Transformative Treatment for PNH
items.content: Eculizumab, introduced in 2007, is a humanized monoclonal antibody that binds to complement component C5. This binding prevents the formation of membrane attack complex (MAC), thereby reducing intravascular hemolysis and its consequences such as hemoglobinuria and thrombosis risk.
keyPoints: Eculizumab significantly reduces or abolishes the need for regular blood transfusions in most patients.
It transforms quality of life by decreasing complications, especially thrombosis.
Administered intravenously every 14 days; Ravelizumab (a derivative) is given at 8-week intervals.
Despite C5 blockade, PNH red cells remain opsonized with C3 fragments, leading to extravascular hemolysis.

items.title: Advancements in Complement Inhibition Beyond C5 Blockade
items.content: To address persistent blood transfusion requirements and extravascular hemolysis in PNH patients on eculizumab, newer agents inhibit complement activation at the C3 level. These drugs prevent both intravascular and extravascular hemolysis.
keyPoints: Pegec-tacoplan (C3 inhibitor) and iptacopan (factor B inhibitor) are approved; danicopan (factor D inhibitor) shows promise in trials.
C3 blockade may completely correct anemia by allowing a larger population of PNH red cells to accumulate, though sudden lysis risk exists if complement blockade is stopped.

items.title: Supportive Management and Considerations for PNH Treatment
items.content: Despite the availability of eculizumab and other complement inhibitors, supportive treatment remains crucial due to cost barriers. This includes folic acid supplementation, periodic serum iron checks, transfusions when necessary, and anticoagulant prophylaxis for thrombosis risk.
keyPoints: Folic acid supplements are mandatory at least 3 mg/d.
Long-term glucocorticoids are not indicated; short courses of prednisone may help during inflammatory exacerbations.
Patients with a history or genetic predisposition to venous thrombosis should receive regular anticoagulant prophylaxis.
For patients with severe PNH or PNH-AA syndrome, BMT or immunosuppressive treatment with antithymocyte globulin and cyclosporine A may be considered.

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.question: What is the main clinical sign that differentiates hemolytic anemias (HAs) from other types of anemia?
items.explanation: The main clinical sign that differentiates HAs from other anemias is jaundice, arising directly from hemolysis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.question: In cases of intravascular hemolysis in hemolytic anemia, which laboratory finding is most indicative?
items.explanation: In intravascular hemolysis, the presence of free hemoglobin and hemoglobinuria is indicative.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.question: Which laboratory parameter reflects the erythropoietic response in hemolytic anemia?
items.explanation: An increased MCV reflects the erythropoietic response, associated with an increase in reticulocytes.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.question: What is a common clinical feature of hemolytic anemia related to the spleen?
items.explanation: The spleen is often enlarged (splenomegaly) because it is a preferential site of hemolysis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.question: What is the final concentration of hemoglobin in mature red cells?
items.explanation: The passage states that the final level of hemoglobin in mature red cells is about 340 g/L.

items.question: Which metabolic process do mature red cells rely on exclusively for ATP production?
items.explanation: The passage explains that anaerobic glycolysis is the only provider of ATP in mature red cells.

items.question: What happens to band 3 molecules as red cells age?
options: They become exposed on the cell surface
items.explanation: The passage mentions that aging and denser red cells have increased probability of band 3 molecules becoming exposed, creating antigenic sites.

items.question: What is the gold standard for proving reduced red cell lifespan?
options: Red cell survival study with $ {}^{51} $Cr or $ {}^{15} $N
items.explanation: The passage identifies red cell survival studies using isotopes like $ {}^{51} $Cr and $ {}^{15} $N as the gold standard for proving reduced lifespan.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism that allows for compensation of increased red cell destruction in hemolysis?
options: Increased production of erythropoietin by the liver
Erythropoiesis mediated by erythropoietin produced by the kidney
Enhanced iron absorption from the gastrointestinal tract
items.explanation: Compensation in hemolysis is achieved through increased erythropoiesis, which is mediated by erythropoietin produced by the kidney.

items.question: In what condition can compensated hemolysis become decompensated and lead to anemia?
options: During periods of high physical activity
In the presence of folate deficiency or renal failure
When there is a decrease in red cell turnover
items.explanation: Compensated hemolysis may become decompensated and lead to anemia during pregnancy, folate deficiency, or renal failure interfering with adequate EPO production.

items.question: What is the effect of an acute infection on erythropoiesis in individuals with chronic hemolytic anemia?
options: It enhances erythropoiesis, leading to increased red cell production
It has no significant impact on erythropoiesis
It depresses erythropoiesis, exacerbating the effects of increased red cell turnover
It stabilizes erythropoiesis, balancing red cell destruction
items.explanation: An acute infection can depress erythropoiesis in individuals with chronic hemolytic anemia, leading to a more marked effect due to increased red cell turnover.

items.question: Which proteins form the main cytoskeletal structure in red blood cells?
items.explanation: The spectrin tetramer, consisting of a head-to-head association of two α-spectrin-β-spectrin heterodimers, is the main cytoskeletal protein.

items.question: What type of inheritance pattern can be observed in severe forms of hereditary spherocytosis?
items.explanation: Some of the most severe forms of hereditary spherocytosis are autosomal recessive.

items.question: What is a common clinical finding in patients with hereditary spherocytosis?
items.explanation: An enlarged spleen is a main clinical finding in patients with hereditary spherocytosis.

items.question: Which diagnostic test was historically used to identify hereditary spherocytosis?
items.explanation: The increase in osmotic fragility became the main diagnostic test for hereditary spherocytosis.

items.question: Which test is used to assess red cell deformability as a function of shear stress at different osmolality in hemolytic anemias?
items.explanation: Ektacytometry is used to assess red cell deformability as a function of shear stress at different osmolality.

items.question: What laboratory finding should raise suspicion for Hereditary Spherocytosis (HS) on an ordinary blood count report?
options: Increased RDW and normal or slightly decreased MCV
items.explanation: An increased mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC >34 g/dL) and increased red cell distribution width (RDW >14%) associated with normal or slightly decreased MCV should raise suspicion of HS.

items.question: What is a possible reason for a negative family history in patients with Hereditary Spherocytosis?
options: The patient has an acquired form of HS
The patient may have a de novo mutation
HS always presents with a positive family history
The patient does not exhibit typical red cell morphology
items.explanation: A negative family history can occur if the patient has a de novo mutation.

items.question: At what age is splenectomy generally recommended for severe cases of hereditary spherocytosis?
items.explanation: Current recommendations are to proceed with splenectomy at the age of 4–6 years in severe cases.

items.question: Which vaccinations are imperative before performing a splenectomy for hereditary spherocytosis?
items.explanation: Before splenectomy, vaccination against encapsulated bacteria (Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae) is imperative.

items.question: What percentage of hereditary spherocytosis cases are estimated to be transfusion dependent?
items.explanation: The most severe cases of HS (estimated at <10%) are transfusion dependent.

items.question: What is the typical mean corpuscular volume (MCV) range in patients with hereditary pyropoikilocytosis?
items.explanation: The red cell volume is decreased (MCV: 50–60 fL) in hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.

items.question: Which technique can differentiate stomatocytosis from spherocytosis by measuring red blood cell deformability?
items.explanation: Ektacytometry measures the deformability of red blood cells and can differentiate stomatocytosis from spherocytosis.

items.question: What is the primary consequence when there is a defect in an enzyme involved in the metabolic machinery of red cells?
options: Direct hemolysis due to compromised cell structure
Hemolysis depending on the enzyme's role
No effect on red blood cells
items.explanation: The consequence depends on the specific role of the defective enzyme in providing energy or preventing oxidative damage.

items.question: What is a potential complication of splenectomy in patients with stomatocytosis?
items.explanation: Splenectomy is contraindicated due to the significant proportion of severe thromboembolic complications observed in splenectomized DHS patients.

items.question: Which treatment has been introduced as an allosteric activator of pyruvate kinase for PK deficiency?
items.explanation: Mitapivat is the first drug approved as an allosteric activator of pyruvate kinase for PK deficiency.

items.question: What procedure may be required in some patients with PK deficiency to manage iron overload?
items.explanation: Iron chelation therapy is used to manage iron overload in patients with PK deficiency.

items.question: What percentage of PK-deficient patients experience a significant increase in hemoglobin after mitapivat treatment?
items.explanation: Mitapivat provides a significant increase in hemoglobin in about one-half of PK-deficient patients.

items.question: Which type of mutation is associated with the effectiveness of mitapivat in treating PK deficiency?
items.explanation: Mitapivat is effective in patients whose PKLR mutations are of the missense type.

items.question: Which of the following best describes why heterozygous females for G6PD deficiency are considered genetic mosaics?
options: They have two different alleles on their X chromosomes.
X chromosome inactivation leads to a variable ratio of normal and deficient cells.
Their phenotype is always intermediate between that of affected males and unaffected females.
The G6PD gene undergoes frequent mutations during cell division.
items.explanation: Heterozygous females are genetic mosaics due to X chromosome inactivation, leading to a variable ratio of G6PD-normal to G6PD-deficient cells.

items.question: Which type of mutation is most commonly associated with G6PD deficiency?
options: Nonsense mutations leading to premature stop codons.
Single missense point mutations resulting in amino acid replacements.
Large chromosomal rearrangements affecting the G6PD gene.
items.explanation: Almost all known mutations leading to G6PD deficiency are single missense point mutations, causing amino acid replacements.

items.question: What is a significant clinical implication of the genetic heterogeneity of G6PD deficiency?
options: It results in uniform clinical manifestations across different populations.
It has no impact on public health.
It leads to varying degrees of enzyme stability and catalytic function.
All individuals with G6PD deficiency experience severe chronic hemolytic anemia.
items.explanation: The genetic heterogeneity affects the degree of enzyme instability and catalytic function, leading to variable clinical manifestations.

items.question: Why is G6PD deficiency considered an example of balanced polymorphism?
options: It causes significant pathology without any benefits.
It confers resistance against Plasmodium falciparum malaria, especially in heterozygotes.
All individuals with the trait have increased susceptibility to infections.
The deficiency is uniformly distributed across all populations.
items.explanation: G6PD deficiency confers a relative resistance against Plasmodium falciparum malaria, especially in heterozygous females, illustrating balanced polymorphism.

items.question: Which of the following is a typical feature seen in G6PD deficiency on a blood film?
items.explanation: The presence of 'bite cells' or 'blister cells' is a typical feature in G6PD deficiency.

items.question: What is the primary reason for the revival in the use of primaquine?
options: It is effective against P. vivax hypnozoites.
It treats acute renal failure caused by AHA.
It prevents transmission of P. falciparum.
items.explanation: Primaquine is used to eliminate gametocytes of P. falciparum, preventing further transmission.

items.question: Which drug should not be administered without G6PD testing due to potential hemolysis?
items.explanation: Rasburicase can cause fatal cases of hemolysis in G6PD-deficient individuals.

items.question: What is the most serious threat from AHA in adults?
items.explanation: The most serious threat from AHA in adults is the development of acute renal failure.

items.question: Which test can definitively diagnose G6PD deficiency in males after a hemolytic attack?
items.explanation: A quantitative test is needed to give a definitive result after a hemolytic attack, as young red cells with higher G6PD activity are released.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary method to prevent favism in individuals with G6PD deficiency?
items.explanation: Favism is entirely preventable by avoiding the consumption of fava beans in G6PD-deficient individuals.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What can be used to diagnose G6PD deficiency besides laboratory tests?
items.explanation: G6PD deficiency can also be diagnosed by DNA testing.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What is the management strategy for non-acute hemolytic anemia (NHA) associated with G6PD deficiency?
options: Avoidance of hemolytic drugs and regular hematologic surveillance
items.explanation: Management includes avoiding exposure to potentially hemolytic drugs and regular hematologic surveillance.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.question: Which gene mutation is NOT commonly associated with familial hemolytic-uremic syndrome (aHUS)?
items.explanation: Mutations in genes such as C3, CFB, and CFI are associated with aHUS; ABO is not mentioned.

items.question: What is the primary cause of hemolysis in familial hemolytic-uremic syndrome?
items.explanation: Hemolysis results from an inherited defect external to red cells, specifically complement system dysregulation.

items.question: What treatment has significantly improved the prognosis of aHUS by inhibiting complement activation?
items.explanation: Eculizumab, an anti-C5 complement inhibitor, has greatly improved the prognosis of aHUS.

items.question: Which statement is true regarding the inheritance pattern of pathogenic variants predisposing to HUS?
items.explanation: Pathogenic variants predispose to HUS with autosomal dominant inheritance and incomplete penetrance.

items.question: Which situation is an example of acute intravascular hemolysis due to mechanical destruction of red cells?
items.explanation: March hemoglobinuria is an acute condition caused by mechanical destruction of red cells during activities like marathon running.

items.question: What chronic and iatrogenic cause of intravascular hemolysis is mentioned in the passage?
items.explanation: Micro-angiopathic hemolytic anemia is a chronic and iatrogenic condition often seen in patients with prosthetic heart valves.

items.question: Which infectious agent is most frequently associated with hemolytic anemia in endemic areas?
items.explanation: In endemic areas, malaria is the most frequent infectious cause of hemolytic anemia.

items.question: What condition can be associated with autoimmune hemolytic anemia upon first presentation or subsequently?
items.explanation: Autoimmune thrombocytopenia can be associated with autoimmune hemolytic anemia, known as Evans syndrome.

items.question: Which reagent is used initially in the direct antiglobulin test to detect immunoglobulins and complement components on red cells?
items.explanation: The direct antiglobulin test initially uses a "broad-spectrum" reagent to detect both immunoglobulins and complement components.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of false-positive results in the direct antiglobulin test?
items.explanation: Infection with Epstein-Barr virus is not mentioned as a common cause of false-positive results in the direct antiglobulin test.

items.question: What is the main mechanism of hemolysis in AIHA?
options: Red cell lysis by free radicals
items.explanation: The main mechanism of hemolysis in AIHA is opsonization and phagocytosis by macrophages recognizing the Fc portion of IgG on red cells.

items.question: What is the initial medical treatment for warm antibody AIHA when anemia is not immediately life-threatening?
options: Transfusion of ABO-matched but incompatible blood
Prednisone at a dose of 1 mg/kg per day
Rituximab at a low dose for four weeks
items.explanation: Prednisone is started immediately when anemia is not life-threatening to produce remission in many patients.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment has been increasingly used as first-line therapy for warm antibody AIHA?
options: Rituximab at a low dose with prednisone
items.explanation: Rituximab, previously second-line, is now used with prednisone as first-line treatment.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.question: What is the rationale for transfusing ABO-matched but incompatible blood in severe acute AIHA?
options: To prevent hemolysis of patient's own red cells
Because it increases compatibility with subsequent units
The transfused cells will be destroyed at a similar rate as the patient's own cells, keeping the patient alive
It cures the underlying autoimmune condition
items.explanation: Transfusing ABO-matched but incompatible blood ensures survival while managing hemolysis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.question: Which statement is true regarding splenectomy in AIHA?
options: It increases the risk of thrombosis and sepsis
It decreases the need for prednisone but does not cure the disease
Both B and C are correct
items.explanation: Splenectomy can reduce the need for other therapies by removing a major site of hemolysis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.question: Which gene mutation is commonly found in the B-cell clone of CAD patients?
items.explanation: A somatic mutation in the KMT2D gene is present in the majority of CAD patients.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.question: What type of antibody does the B-cell clone produce in cold agglutinin disease?
items.explanation: The antibody produced by the B-cell clone in CAD is IgM.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.question: Which treatment has become de facto first-line for CAD due to its effectiveness?
items.explanation: Rituximab is the de facto first-line treatment for CAD.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following treatments is ineffective in CAD?
items.explanation: Unlike AIHA, prednisone and splenectomy are ineffective in CAD.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.question: Which drug is known to stimulate the production of the Rhesus antibody anti-e, leading to true autoimmune hemolytic anemia?
items.explanation: Methyldopa can trigger the production of the Rhesus antibody anti-e, causing true AIHA.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation for patients with Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH) when seeking medical attention?
items.explanation: Patients with PNH may report passing blood instead of urine, known as the classic presentation.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.question: What is a typical laboratory finding in patients with Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria?
items.explanation: In PNH, LDH is typically markedly elevated.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.question: What can cause hemolysis to subside in patients with penicillin-induced AIHA?
items.explanation: Hemolysis subsides once penicillin administration is stopped in penicillin-induced AIHA.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.question: Which test is considered the gold standard for diagnosing PNH today?
items.explanation: The passage states that flow cytometry is the gold standard for diagnosing PNH.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.question: What percentage of total red cells must be CD59 and CD55 negative to diagnose PNH?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that at least 5% of the total red cells must be CD59 and CD55 negative for a PNH diagnosis.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.question: What is the primary cause of hemolysis in PNH?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that hemolysis in PNH is mainly intravascular due to an intrinsic abnormality making red cells sensitive to activated complement.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.question: What genetic mutation is primarily responsible for the deficiency of protective proteins on red cell membranes in PNH?
items.explanation: The passage identifies a somatic mutation in the X-linked PIGA gene as responsible for GPI deficiency, leading to lack of protective proteins.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.question: Which monoclonal antibody introduced in 2007 binds to the complement component C5 to treat PNH?
items.explanation: Eculizumab is the monoclonal antibody introduced in 2007 that binds to C5.

items.question: What is a consequence of blocking C5 with eculizumab in PNH patients?
options: Reduction in intravascular hemolysis and thrombosis risk
items.explanation: Blocking C5 with eculizumab reduces intravascular hemolysis and the risk of thrombosis.

items.question: Why might PNH patients on eculizumab still require blood transfusions?
options: Eculizumab causes extravascular hemolysis due to C3 opsonization.
Eculizumab increases the production of defective red cells.
Eculizumab leads to iron deficiency anemia.
items.explanation: PNH patients on eculizumab may still require transfusions due to extravascular hemolysis from C3 opsonization.

items.question: Which treatment option is considered for PNH patients with severe disease and an HLA-identical sibling available?
items.explanation: Bone marrow transplantation is considered for severe PNH patients with an HLA-identical sibling.

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.statement: In hemolytic anemia, the bone marrow's erythropoietic response is typically reflected by a decreased reticulocyte count.
items.explanation: The erythropoietic response in HAs is usually indicated by an increased reticulocyte count.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.statement: In hemolytic anemia, jaundice results from the overconsumption of red cells in peripheral blood.
items.explanation: Jaundice arises directly from hemolysis due to overconsumption of red cells.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.statement: Mature red cells can replace their protein components if they deteriorate.
items.explanation: The passage states that mature red cells cannot replace deteriorated proteins due to the loss of ribosomes.

items.statement: Chronic extravascular hemolysis often leads to iron overload.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that chronic extravascular hemolysis can lead to iron overload, especially with frequent blood transfusions.

items.statement: Compensated hemolysis always results in anemia.
items.explanation: In compensated hemolysis, the increased red cell production balances the destruction, so there is no anemia.

items.statement: Parvovirus B19 infection can cause a precipitous fall in hemoglobin in individuals with chronic hemolytic anemia.
items.explanation: Infection by parvovirus B19 may lead to an aplastic crisis, causing a significant drop in hemoglobin levels.

items.statement: Hereditary elliptocytosis is characterized by a linear-shaped central pale area in red cells.
items.explanation: The linear-shaped central pale area in red cells characterizes stomatocytosis, not hereditary elliptocytosis.

items.statement: Hereditary spherocytosis can arise from mutations in several different genes.
items.explanation: HS is genetically heterogeneous and can arise from a variety of mutations in one of several genes.

items.statement: The spleen is not involved in the pathophysiology of Hereditary Spherocytosis.
items.explanation: The spleen plays a key role in HS through mechanisms including increased red cell destruction.

items.statement: A definitive diagnosis of Hereditary Spherocytosis can sometimes only be obtained by molecular studies.
items.explanation: Molecular studies demonstrating a mutation in one of the genes underlying HS may be required for a definitive diagnosis.

items.statement: Splenectomy should be avoided in mild cases of hereditary spherocytosis.
items.explanation: Current recommendations are to avoid splenectomy in mild cases.

items.statement: The Southeast Asia ovalocytosis (SAO) is considered a disease rather than a polymorphism.
items.explanation: SAO is not a disease, but rather a polymorphism with a frequency of up to 5–7% in certain populations.

items.statement: Pyruvate kinase deficiency is an autosomal dominant disease.
items.explanation: Pyruvate kinase deficiency is an autosomal recessive disease.

items.statement: The glycolytic pathway in red cells provides ATP necessary for cation transport across the membrane.
items.explanation: Red cells rely on the anaerobic portion of the glycolytic pathway to produce ATP required for cation transport.

items.statement: Splenectomy often results in a decrease in hemoglobin levels for PK-deficient patients.
items.explanation: Splenectomy usually provides a modest but significant increase in hemoglobin levels.

items.statement: Bone marrow transplantation has been successfully used for some PK-deficient patients with severe disease.
items.explanation: BMT from an HLA-identical PK-normal sibling has been successful in treating severe PK deficiency.

items.statement: Males with G6PD deficiency are hemizygous for the gene.
items.explanation: Males have only one X chromosome, so they are hemizygous for the G6PD gene.

items.statement: G6PD deficiency is primarily caused by large chromosomal rearrangements.
items.explanation: G6PD deficiency is caused by single missense point mutations, not large chromosomal rearrangements.

items.statement: Fava bean pollen inhalation causes favism.
items.explanation: Contrary to widespread beliefs, fava bean pollen inhalation does not cause favism.

items.statement: G6PD deficiency is associated with normocytic and normochromic anemia.
items.explanation: AHA due to G6PD deficiency typically presents as moderate to extremely severe, normocytic and normochromic anemia.

items.statement: In cases of acute renal failure due to G6PD deficiency, hemodialysis is always necessary.
items.explanation: Hemodialysis may be necessary if there is acute renal failure, but recovery is the rule if there's no previous kidney disease.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.statement: Splenic destruction is a known cause of red cell destruction in G6PD deficiency.
items.explanation: There is no evidence of selective red cell destruction in the spleen for G6PD deficiency.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.statement: Plasma exchange is the only effective treatment for aHUS.
items.explanation: Eculizumab has become an effective alternative to plasma exchange in treating aHUS.

items.statement: aHUS can undergo spontaneous remission.
items.explanation: The passage states that aHUS not infrequently undergoes spontaneous remission.

items.statement: The mortality rate for AIHA is approximately 5-10% even with appropriate management.
items.explanation: AIHA has a mortality rate of about 5-10% despite proper treatment.

items.statement: Micro-angiopathic hemolytic anemia is typically acute and self-inflicted.
items.explanation: Micro-angiopathic hemolytic anemia is chronic and iatrogenic, not acute and self-inflicted.

items.statement: The direct antiglobulin test is positive if there are at least 400 molecules of Ig and/or C per red cell.
items.explanation: The test is generally considered positive with an average of at least 400 molecules of Ig and/or C on each red cell.

items.statement: AIHA can only be diagnosed when the direct antiglobulin test is positive.
items.explanation: Coombs-negative AIHA can occur, where severe AIHA may be present despite a negative Coombs test.

items.statement: Rituximab is used as a second-line treatment in warm antibody AIHA.
items.explanation: Rituximab is increasingly used at low doses with prednisone as first-line therapy.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.statement: Intravenous immunoglobulin is a third-line agent in the treatment of AIHA.
items.explanation: IVIG has become a second- or third-line agent after rituximab's introduction.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.statement: The antibody involved in cold agglutinin disease reacts strongly at body temperature.
items.explanation: The autoantibody in CAD reacts poorly or not at all at 37°C.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.statement: Hemolysis in CAD is primarily caused by splenic destruction of red cells.
items.explanation: In CAD, the spleen is not predominant; hemolysis occurs via complement activation and macrophage action.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.statement: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria can present with recurrent attacks of severe abdominal pain due to thrombosis in abdominal veins.
items.explanation: PNH may present with severe abdominal pain related to thrombosis in abdominal veins.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.statement: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria is an inherited disease.
items.explanation: PNH is not inherited and has never been reported as a congenital disease.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.statement: Thrombosis is a well-understood complication in the pathogenesis of PNH.
items.explanation: The passage notes that thrombosis is one of the least understood complications in PNH.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.statement: PIGA mutations are found only in patients with PNH.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that PIGA mutations can be demonstrated in normal people as well.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.statement: Eculizumab completely prevents all forms of hemolysis in PNH patients.
items.explanation: Eculizumab prevents intravascular hemolysis but not extravascular hemolysis due to C3 opsonization.

items.statement: Long-term glucocorticoids are recommended for managing chronic hemolysis in PNH.
items.explanation: Long-term glucocorticoids are not indicated and are contraindicated due to side effects.

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.assertion: In hemolytic anemia, the presence of macrocytes on a blood smear reflects increased erythropoiesis.
items.reason: Increased reticulocyte count leads to an increase in mean corpuscular volume (MCV).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.subtopic: Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES
items.assertion: In hemolytic anemia, intravascular hemolysis is indicated by elevated haptoglobin levels.
items.reason: Free hemoglobin from lysed red cells binds to haptoglobin in the blood.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because intravascular hemolysis results in reduced, not elevated, haptoglobin levels.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Hemolytic Anemias | DEFINITIONS | GENERAL CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.assertion: Increased red cell turnover is the essential pathophysiologic process common to all hemolytic anemias.
items.reason: The senescence of red cells is accelerated in various ways, leading to increased destruction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains that acceleration of senescence leads to increased turnover.

items.assertion: Chronic intravascular hemolysis results in iron overload.
items.reason: Persistent hemoglobinuria causes considerable iron loss, needing replacement.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that chronic intravascular hemolysis leads to iron loss.

items.assertion: Compensated hemolysis can become decompensated during pregnancy.
items.reason: Pregnancy may interfere with adequate erythropoietin production, leading to anemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why compensated hemolysis can become decompensated during pregnancy.

items.assertion: Hereditary spherocytosis is always inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
items.reason: The inheritance of HS can also be autosomal recessive, particularly in severe forms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hereditary spherocytosis can have autosomal recessive inheritance.

items.assertion: Spectrin tetramers are essential for maintaining red cell shape.
items.reason: The cytoskeleton, including spectrin tetramers, supports membrane stability and deformability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The shape of red cells gives the name to hereditary elliptocytosis.
items.reason: There is no direct correlation between elliptocytic morphology and clinical severity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the shape names the condition.

items.assertion: Mutations in PIEZO1 or KCCN4 cause hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.
items.reason: These mutations result in decreased tetramerization of spectrum dimers.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; mutations causing HPP are not in PIEZO1 or KCCN4, but rather involve genes affecting cytoskeleton stability.

items.assertion: Prenatal diagnosis of PK deficiency can be carried out.
items.reason: A mother who had already had an affected child underwent prenatal diagnosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: G6PD deficiency is primarily due to intracorpuscular causes.
items.reason: Hemolysis in G6PD deficiency is usually triggered by exogenous agents.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because G6PD deficiency involves both intracorpuscular and extracorpuscular causes.

items.assertion: Heterozygous females for G6PD deficiency can exhibit clinical symptoms similar to hemizygous males.
items.reason: This is due to the variable ratio of G6PD-normal to G6PD-deficient cells caused by X chromosome inactivation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why heterozygous females can be as affected as hemizygous males.

items.assertion: The prevalence of G6PD deficiency is due to its protective effect against malaria.
items.reason: This protection is conferred by the presence of homozygous mutations in the G6PD gene.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason is false because it is heterozygotes who are protected, not individuals with homozygous mutations.

items.assertion: Patients with CNSHA associated with G6PD deficiency are vulnerable to acute oxidative damage.
items.reason: The same agents that cause AHA in ordinary G6PD deficiency can exacerbate hemolysis in CNSHA patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Primaquine is necessary for treating life-threatening P. falciparum malaria.
items.reason: It is the only effective agent for eliminating gametocytes of P. falciparum.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; primaquine is not necessary for treating life-threatening P. falciparum malaria, but it is used to prevent transmission.

items.assertion: Favism can be prevented by avoiding fava beans.
items.reason: G6PD-deficient individuals are susceptible to hemolysis when exposed to favism triggers like fava beans.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.assertion: Splenectomy is routinely performed in all cases of G6PD deficiency.
items.reason: Splenic destruction contributes to red cell destruction in G6PD deficiency.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; splenectomy is not routine, and the reason is also false as there's no evidence of selective red cell destruction by the spleen.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 3) | G6PD Deficiency (part 1)

items.assertion: Eculizumab can be discontinued after achieving full remission in aHUS.
items.reason: Once urine dipstick is negative for hemoglobinuria within 3–6 months, eculizumab can be stopped.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: All mutations identified by NGS in inherited red cell abnormalities have clear significance.
items.reason: NGS provides definitive data for diagnosing hereditary HAs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because some mutations may be of uncertain significance; the reason is true.

items.assertion: Evans syndrome signals high-risk disease.
items.reason: It is associated with autoimmune thrombocytopenia and AIHA, indicating a common variable immune deficiency or primary immune deficiency syndromes in children.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; Evans syndrome indicates high-risk disease due to its association with multiple autoimmune conditions.

items.assertion: March hemoglobinuria is a chronic condition.
items.reason: It results from prolonged mechanical destruction of red cells during activities like marathon running.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; march hemoglobinuria is an acute condition, not chronic.

items.assertion: Prednisone is used as an initial treatment for warm antibody AIHA.
items.reason: It produces remission promptly in at least one-half of patients when anemia is not life-threatening.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why prednisone is used initially.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.assertion: Splenectomy cures warm antibody AIHA.
items.reason: It removes a major site of hemolysis, improving anemia and reducing other therapy needs.
items.explanation: While splenectomy improves the condition, it does not cure AIHA.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 1)

items.assertion: Rituximab has become the first-line treatment for CAD.
items.reason: Previously used immunosuppressive/cytotoxic agents have limited clinical efficacy and unacceptable side effects.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.assertion: Prednisone is effective in treating CAD.
items.reason: In CAD, prednisone can reduce antibody titer and manage symptoms effectively.
items.explanation: A is false; prednisone is ineffective in CAD.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 2)

items.assertion: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria can lead to acute myeloid leukemia.
items.reason: PNH may terminate in acute myeloid leukemia, although it is rare.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.assertion: The most common cause of death in PNH is infection secondary to severe neutropenia.
items.reason: Venous thrombosis is the most common cause of death in patients with PNH.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but venous thrombosis is the more common cause of death.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 3)

items.assertion: PNH results from the combined action of failure of normal hematopoiesis and massive expansion of a PNH clone.
items.reason: Expansion is associated with negative selection against GPI-positive cells by GPI-specific T cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why PNH results from these combined actions.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.assertion: The deficiency of CD59 on platelets is the only cause of thrombosis in PNH.
items.reason: Deficiency of CD59 on PNH platelets causes inappropriate platelet activation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because other mechanisms are possible for thrombosis, though the reason is true.
items.reference: Hemolytic Anemias · Warm Antibody Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (part 4)

items.assertion: C3 blockade has a greater chance than C5 blockade to completely correct anemia in PNH.
items.reason: C3 blockade prevents both intravascular and extravascular hemolysis by inhibiting complement activation upstream.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why C3 blockade can more completely correct anemia.

items.assertion: Immunosuppressive treatment with antithymocyte globulin and cyclosporine A is ineffective for PNH.
items.reason: These treatments are used in conjunction with eculizumab for patients with severe PNH or PNH-AA syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these immunosuppressive treatments can be effective, especially when combined with eculizumab.
